coredata.dc:title: Effect of electron-donating/withdrawing substituents on the fluorescence of aniline containing fluorescein Schiff base derivatives
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jinxia
Yan, Fanyong
Sun, Xiaodong
Wang, Zhaoxu
coredata.dc:description: Two Schiff bases fluorescence sensors (FP1 and FP2) based on fluorescein-monoaldehyde derivatives were designed, synthesized and characterized. The effect of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents on fluorescence of fluorescein was investigated. Through the calculation of the frontier orbital, it is proved that when the probe coexists with Cu2+, the complex system is more stable and easier to form. The FP1 was used as effective fluorescent contrast agent for determining Cu2+ from 0 to 55 μM with the limit of detection of 0.23 μM in several real water samples, sensing membranes and intracellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescein
Schiff base
Aniline
Electron-donating substituent
Electron-withdrawing substituent
Copper ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing behavior of FP1 towards metal ions
Recognition mechanism for on-off switching behavior of FP1
Application of EDS functionalized fluorescein Schiff base FP1 to water samples, sensing membranes and living cells
Comparison of FP1 with previously reported fluorescent sensors for detection of Cu2+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
National Natural Science Foundation of China
Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed mechanism between FP1 and Cu2+.

(a) Absorption spectra and (b) fluorescence spectra of FP1 (10 and 1 μM, respectively) with addition of different metal ions in aqueous solution (Tris-HCl buffer, 10 μM, pH 7.4) with an excitation at 440 nm. The intensity of maximum absorption peak was at 488 nm and the maximum fluorescence intensity was at 506 nm. (c): The fluorescence spectra of FP1 (10 μM) upon the addition of different concentration of Cu2+. Insert: plot of fluorescence intensity ratios of F0-F at 506 nm versus the concentration of Cu2+ in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4). (d): Evolution of CIE chromaticity coordinates of FP1 for the detection of Cu2+ at different concentrations derived from fluorescence spectra. λex=440 nm.

(a) Red bar: The fluorescence intensity of FP1 (1 μM) in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 μM, pH 7. 4) after addition of various metal ions. Black bars: Fluorescence intensity of FP1-Cu2+ with other metal ions. (b)Variation of fluorescence spectra of FP1 (10 μM) in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 μM, pH 7. 4) with and without (4 equiv.) Cu2+ ion as a function of pH. Fluorescence intensity at 506 nm.
Frontier molecular orbital profiles of FP1 and complex FP1-Cu2+ as well as ligand optimized under DFT.
Photographs of sensor-loaded sensing membrane for the detection of Cu2+.
Bright field and fluorescence imaging of VSMCs cells stained with FP1 (a, c), stained with FP1/Cu2+ (b, d).
Detection of Cu2+ spiked in tap water and real lake water samples.
Comparison of fluorescent probe platforms for the detection of Cu2+.
The photoluminescence quantum yields (ΦF) for FP1 and FP2 and fluorescein in Tris-HCl, pH 7.4, at 25 °C.

head.ce:title: Effect of electron-donating/withdrawing substituents on the fluorescence of aniline containing fluorescein Schiff base derivatives

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, 411201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of FP1 for the detection of Cu2+

Two Schiff bases fluorescence sensors (FP1 and FP2) based on fluorescein-monoaldehyde derivatives were designed, synthesized and characterized. The effect of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents on fluorescence of fluorescein was investigated. Through the calculation of the frontier orbital, it is proved that when the probe coexists with Cu2+, the complex system is more stable and easier to form. The FP1 was used as effective fluorescent contrast agent for determining Cu2+ from 0 to 55 μM with the limit of detection of 0.23 μM in several real water samples, sensing membranes and intracellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+) is a significant environmental pollutant by entering food chain with a potentially negative impact on human health [1–3]. Development of high sensitivity and selective sensors for Cu2+ is of considerable importance in the fields of biological, chemical and environmental sciences. A variety of efficient sensors for Cu2+, including naphthalene, benzothiazole, benzopyran, BODIPY, xanthene, have been reported in recent years [4–7]. As a classic fluorophore, fluorescein and its derivatives possess unique structural advantages, and accompanied by a large rigid conjugate system with excellent spectral characteristics [8–10]. Accordingly, some molecular sensors based on fluorescein derivatives have been developed for detection Cu2+ [11]. Nevertheless, owing to the inherent fluorescence quenching property that originated in the paramagnetic nature of Cu2+, previously much effort has been devoted to the development of sensors which generally display decrease emission upon binding with Cu2+, studies on the fluorescence properties of substituents on sensors are relatively rare.

In fact, the manipulation of electronic features of substituents in fluorescein backbones may lead to a change in fluorescence properties [12,13]. The transition allowance is dominated by the symmetry of the molecular orbitals related to a corresponding transition. Local electric ﬁelds can change the transition moment between ground and excited states and, therefore, affect the transition allowance and strength. The dipole moment increases by increasing local ﬁeld in the same direction. These changings way will inﬂuence the absorption spectrum of fluorescence dyes [14,15]. The electronic substituent increases intersystem crossing from S1 to T1, and the number of photons emitted by S1-S0 greatly decreases, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence [18]. It is fully realized that the design of such fluorescein derivatives is a challenging task because of the different chemical profiles of electron-withdrawing substituents (EWS) or electron-donating substituents (EDS). Fluorescent sensors based on fluorescence aldehyde have been synthesized and applied to detect various ions [16–22]. However, there have been no studies on the effect of fluorescein aldehydes on fluorescein properties after reacting with anilines containing electron-withdrawing or electron donating groups. One typical strategy is the used fluorescein bearing EWS, or EDS, leads to the dramatic fluorescence intensity variety by changing push-pull system. Based on this strategy, we envisioned that incorporation of the EWS or EDS into the fluorescein fluorophore, which could specifically respond to the sample, would lead to a new fluorescent sensor for detecting or quantifying a molecular target in the sample.

In this work, two different fluorescein-based Schiff bases were synthesized by coupling fluorescein mono-aldehyde and anilines with either EDS or EWS in various positions bound to aromatic ring. Then their UV–vis, optical, fluorescence properties were investigated, the effect of the substituent positions was discussed, and the relationship between their structures and properties were summarized. Because the methyl groups contained in FP1 increased the binding selectivity of FP1 to Cu2+, while FP2 didn’t show a good selectivity for cations and ionisable analytes. The density functional theory (DFT) calculations for FP1, FP1-Cu and FP2 are carried out via the Gaussian program [23–26]. Our experimental results demonstrated that modification with EDS could synergistically improve sensitive and specific detection of fluorescein Schiff base derivatives and have a range of promising chemical sensing and bioanalysis applications.

The material used such as Fluorescein, 2, 4-dimethylaniline, 4-Fluoro-3-nitroaniline was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. China. Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, China. Stock solutions of metal ions were prepared by dissolving their nitrates in water. High purity metal nitrates were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent, China. All chemicals used were of at least analytical grade and used as received without further purification unless otherwise stated. Ultrapure water obtained from Milipore Mili-Q Advantage-A10 and Milipore Elix-5 water purification system (˜18.2 MΩ.cm, 25 °C) was used throughout the study. 1000 Da MWCO cellulose dialysis membrane was bought from Membrane Filtration Product.

All fluorescence measurements were obtained using an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The samples were diluted with appropriate amount of ultrapure water and transferred to a four-side clear quartz cuvette with a path length of 1.0 cm for fluorescence measurement. Excitation and emission slits were both set at 10 nm throughout the study. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a Purkinje General TU-1901 spectrometer. The absorbance measurements were recorded within the range of 200 nm–800 nm. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained in KBr disks on a TENSOR37 spectrometer. 1 H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were measured with Bruker Advance IIWB 400 MHz spectrometer.

FP1 and FP2 were both prepared in two steps from fluorescein, as shown in Scheme 1 . Exactly 10 ml of ethanol solution of 2, 4-dimethylaniline (97.0 mg, 0. 800 mmol) was added to a slurry of fluorescein-monoaldehyde (200 mg, 0. 560 mmol) in 30 ml ethanol. Then 10 drops of glacial acetic acid were added to the solution, the mixture was stirred and refluxed for about 36 h. After cooling to the room temperature, the solution was removed solvent. Then the powder was further purified with Column chromatography. Column separation with 1: 20 methanol: methylene chloride. As a result, the system gave the final product as red powder (250 mg). Yield: 53.2%. 1H NMR (d-DMSO, 400 MHz) δ 9.78 (s, 1 H), 9.31 (s, 1 H), 8.01 – 7.85 (m, 2 H), 7.76 – 7.57 (m, 3 H), 7.25 (d, J =8.0 Hz, 1 H), 7.15 (d, J =7.6 Hz, 1 H), 7.11 – 7.05 (m, 2 H), 6.78 (d, J =1.9 Hz, 1 H), 6.65 (d, J =8.9 Hz, 1 H), 6.60 – 6.56 (m, 1 H), 6.53 – 6.49 (m, 1 H), 2.31 (d, J =4.8 Hz, 6 H). 13C NMR (d-DMSO, 100 MHz) δ = 185.7, 167.6, 161.0, 160.0, 156.9, 153.2, 148.1, 147.9, 139.8, 134.3, 133.8, 132.2, 133.2, 130.8, 129.0, 128.7, 128.7, 128.5, 128.4, 127.8, 127.3, 122.1, 115.3, 114.8, 112.2, 108.5, 95.3, 21.6, 18.6 ppm. HRMS (ESI): [M+H]+ Calcd for FP1 464.1492; Found, 464.1552. Elemental Analysis: Calcd for FP1 C, 75.15; H, 4.57; N, 3.02; O, 17.26; Found, C, 75.24; H, 4.51; N, 3.03; O, 17.29. The synthesis steps of FP2 are similar with the synthesis steps of FP1. And after purification, a yellow solid (80.0 mg) was obtained. Yield: 76.1%. 1H NMR (d-DMSO, 400 MHz) δ 14.01 (s, 1 H), 11.89 (s, 1 H), 10.63 (s, 1 H), 10.22 (s, 1 H), 9.57 (s, 1 H), 8.40 (dd, J = 6.6, 2.6 Hz, 1 H), 8.02 (d, J = 3.9 Hz, 1 H), 7.82 (d, J =7.0 Hz, 1 H), 7.75 (m, 1 H), 7.31 (dd, J = 7.6, 3.6 Hz, 1 H), 7.22 (dd, J = 4.1, 1.9 Hz, 1 H), 6.97 (d, J =2.8 Hz, 1 H), 6.83 (s, 1 H), 6.72 (m, 1 H), 6.61 (s, 2 H). 13C NMR (d-DMSO, 100 MHz) δ = 182.7, 169.5, 167.6, 158.4, 156.8, 156.7, 155.1, 153.4, 147.9, 139.8, 135.4, 134.3, 133.8, 132.2, 129.0, 128.5, 128.5, 128.4, 127.8, 126.8, 118.8, 116.4, 110.5, 110.0, 108.4, 101.7, 100.8 ppm. MS (ESI): M Calcd for FP2 498.09; Found, 498.54. Elemental Analysis: Calcd for FP2 C, 65.06; H, 3.03; N, 5.62; O, 22.47; Found C, 65.10; H, 3.01; N, 5.69; O, 22.41.

A stock solution of FP1 (1 × 10−3 M) and FP2 (1 × 10−3 M) were prepared in methanol, stock solutions of metal ions (Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, La3+, Ce3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Na+, 2 × 10−3 M) were prepared by dissolving their nitrate salts in double distilled water. All the test solutions were prepared by the following procedure. A 0.1 mL stock solution of FP1 and FP2 were both added to 10 mL volumetric flasks directly with a micropipette then the suitable amounts of metal ions were added and constant volume with water solution (Tris-HCl buffer, 10 μM, pH 7.4), afterwards, the mixtures were incubated over 120 min. All experimental solutions were prepared at room temperature. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with both excitation and emission slits are set to 5.0 nm.

The detection limit (3 σ/k) was calculated based on the fluorescence titration, where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, k is the slope of the fit line in Fig. 1 c. To determine σ, the emission intensity of FP1 in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4) without any metal ions was measured 6 times, respectively.

DFT calculations for ground state were performed at M06-2X/6-311 g(d) level, and TD-DFT calculations for the first excited state using long range corrected functional CAM-B3LYP method. All the calculations were carried out via the Gaussian 09 suites of program [27].

The application of copper ion detection was studied in two water samples from tap water (Tianjin, China) and Pan Lake water (Tianjin, China). Two water samples were filtered through 0.22 μM membrane (Millipore) before used. Then, 0.1 mL stock solution of FP1 was mixed with 10 mL of above water samples, and different concentrations of Cu2+ solution. Afterwards, the mixtures were incubated over 120 min. All experimental solutions were prepared at room temperature. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with both excitation and emission slits are set to 5.0 nm.

VSMCs cells were obtained and cultured in the in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DEME) which contains 10% fetal bovine serum and was incubated in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air at 37 °C. The cells were dispersed into a single cell suspension by parenzyme and then cultured with a six-well plate After 24 h, the medium was discarded, and the FP1 solution was added to continue the incubation for 30 min and then imaged under an inverted fluorescence microscope. For the Cu2+ treated samples, the cells were washed three times to remove extracellular free FP1, then Cu2+ was added and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. All the cells were washed twice with PBS, followed by addition of 1 mL DMEM culture medium, before observation under a fluorescence microscope.

The synthetic route is shown in Scheme 1. First, an aldehyde functionalized fluorescein was synthesized according to previously reported method [28]. The further reaction of mono-formylated fluorescein with 2, 4-dimethylaniline in ethanol afforded sensor FP1 (Scheme 1). For a control experiment, FP2 was prepared by the similar way with 4-Fluoro-3-nitroaniline. Their structures were conﬁrmed by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, FT-IR and mass spectrometry. The detailed characterization data of two sensors were presented in the experimental section and supplementary materials.

The formation of Schiff base derivative of FP1 was confirmed by the appearance of the characteristic HCN band at about 1620 cm−1 in its FT-IR spectra. The synthesis of FP1 was also confirmed by the appearance of imine protons (HCN) at δ 8.72 ppm in the 1H NMR spectra. The details of the characterization data of compound FP1 were loaded on the Supporting Information (Fig.S1-S3). The details of the characterization data of compound FP2 were loaded on the Supporting Information (Fig.S4-S6). In Fig.S7 (UV–vis spectrum of FP1 and FP2), initial FP1 and FP2 displayed xanthene moiety based characteristic absorption peak at 331 nm. The ring-opening fluorescein based characteristic absorption peak (493 nm) was also found.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing behavior of FP1 towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the selectivity of FP1 and FP2 were investigated toward Cu2+ and other species, the emission spectra of the compounds in the presence of different metal ions were obtained. In Fig. 1b the fluorescence intensity of FP1 is significantly reduced after adding Cu2+, however fluorescence intensity of FP2 has not changed after adding Cu2+ (Fig. S8). Results showed that the selectivity of FP2 is inferior to FP1’s in that different density of electron clouds, which caused by the different substituents on aniline. In FP1 and FP2, because of the substituent on aniline is farther away from the fluorescein ring, the substituent effect is less obvious, so the fluorescence intensity of the two is not much different [29–31]. Therefore, the influence of EDS (EWS) on the density of benzene ring electrons on aniline is mainly considered. To adjust the reacting activity of the probes, we substituted different pushing–drawing electron groups on the fluorescein backbone bridging with anilines to increase the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). EDS could enhance the ICT effect and promote the reaction activity of the FP1 toward Cu2+, thus leading to more selective. The case of fluorine and nitro is the opposite. In the following, further researches were mainly carried on FP1.

Fig. 1 showed the varieties of absorption and fluorescence spectra of FP1 after addition of different metal ions in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 μM, pH 7.4). In stark contrast to other metal cations, when 4 equivalents of Cu2+ were added, the absorption band centered at 488 nm appeared with increasing intensity, clearly suggesting the formation of FP1 upon Cu2+ binding, the color of the solution of FP1 turned to light green from green. The maximum fluorescence emission peak 506 nm almost quenched. It is reasonable to assume that Cu2+ can easily bind to FP1 through phenoxido oxygens and imino nitrogens, Cu2+ is a paramagnetic ion with d9 configuration, resulting in the reduction of the fluorescence. The addition of other metal ions (Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, La3+, Ce3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+ and Na+) to the FP1 solution, under the same conditions, no obvious absorption and fluorescence changes in FP1 were found (Fig. 1a, b). The measurement of the emission maximum 506 nm of FP1-Cu2+ showed that the fluorescence intensity of FP1 and Cu2+in the concentration range of 0.1–210 μM exhibits a good linear relationship. The linear relationship can be represented by the relationship between common [F0-F] and [Cu2+] conforming to the equation: Y = 14.65X +59.19. (R2 = 0.996). F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of FP1 in the presence and absence of Cu2+. Moreover, the detection limit of Cu2+ was estimated to be 0.23 μM (Fig. 1c). The fluorescence titration data was also used to study the color characteristics of fluorescence from FP1 by using CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates. Meanwhile the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram for FP1 with increasing concentrations of Cu2+ was measured, as shown in Fig. 1d [32]. The CIE coordinates varied between (0.20, 0.65), (0.20, 0.63), (0.21, 0.62) and (0.22, 0.62) for the concentrations of Cu2+ range from 0 μM to 55 μM, corresponding to hues ranging in green region (Fig. 2 b). The absorbance titration profile was also studied; a positive correlation relationship was observed between the absorbance of FP1 and the concentration of Cu2+ (0–400 μM). Detailed absorption changes were shown in Fig.S9.

To further evaluate the selectivity of FP1 for Cu2+ the competitive experiments was conducted. And the influence of pH on the fluorescence emission features of FP1 toward the detection of Cu2+ was also examined in Tris-HCl buffer solution, respectively. In Fig. 2 (a), only the introduction of Cu2+ lead to a remarkable decrease of fluorescence at 506 nm, which obviously implied the high selectivity was then conducted by putting Cu2+ into FP1 solution containing other metal ions. The results demonstrated that FP1 can effectively detect Cu2+ in the presence of other interfering metal ions [33]. And pH effects were essential for application of the sensor. Thus, the effect of pH on the detection of Cu2+ by FP1 has been studied. Fig. 2(b) showed that in the aqueous solution, the appropriate pH range for the determination of Cu2+ is pH 5.7–9.0, where the florescence “on-off” can be functioned via the binding of Cu2+. When the pH > 9.0, cause the generate of Cu(OH)2, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor would not decrease. This result indicates that FP1 can work beneath the characteristic physiological pH conditions and therefore has probable for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition mechanism for on-off switching behavior of FP1
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the binding stoichiometry, a Job’s plot analysis was studied to get the information about of FP1-Cu2+ complexation in Tris-HCl (10 μM, pH 7.4) buffer solution under the optimized conditions [34]. The binding mode of FP1 and Cu2+ is most likely as a 2: 1 stoichiometry in accordance with a maximum fluorescence emission at 506 nm when the molecular fraction of Cu2+ was near to 30% (Fig. S10(a)) [35]. The result suggested that FP1 and Cu2+ was coordinated by 2: 1 stoichiometry. In Fig. S10(b), the fluorescence intensity of FP1 was obviously quenched (about 4.75-fold) after addition of Cu2+ in 10 min. By metal ion coordination, the electron was transferred from fluorescein unit to Cu2+ occurrence [36]. The fluorescence-quenching behavior corresponds to the charge transfer from the excited state of FP1 to Cu2+ center and thus provide a pathway for the nonradiative deactivation of the excited state. The mechanism is shown in Scheme 2 .

The frontier orbitals energy of FP1 and FP1-Cu2+ were calculated to explore Cu2+ how effect on the probe (Fig. 3 ). DFT calculations for ground state were performed at M06-2X/6-311 g(d) level via the Gaussian 09 suites of program. HOMO (-6.657 eV) of the probe distributes on methylaniline and LUMO (-1.802 eV) mainly concentrates in the xanthene of fluorescein. The energy level difference of FP1 is 4.855 eV. The energy of MOs becomes lower with the formation of complex systems, new HOMO (-11.337 eV) located near Cu2+ and new LUMO (-6.441 eV) transfer to the xanthene of one of the fluorescein. The energy level difference of complexed system tends to become 4.896 eV of FP1-Cu2+. Through the calculation of the frontier orbital, it is proved that when the probe coexists with Cu2+, the complex system is more stable and easier to form [37–40]. Frontier molecular orbital profiles of FP2 are shown in Fig. S11.

The reversibility is vital for cost-effectiveness of the sensors. Results showed that FP1 retained reversible properties via using EDTA as the reversing reagent. In the research, Cu2+ and EDTA were added to FP1 solution in turn whereas the absorption and fluorescence spectra were both documented. By adding Cu2+ to the solution of FP1 induced increasing of the absorbance intensity at 488 nm and the quenching of the fluorescence emission at 506 nm, respectively [41]. Furthermore, the absorption and emissions of FP1-Cu2+ recovered to the initial intensities when excess EDTA was added, the sensing process was considered to be reversible. The results indicating the reversible properties of FP1 for sensing Cu2+ ions (Fig. S12). According to all the results, FP1 could be recycled for detecting Cu2+, making it valuable for commercial uses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of EDS functionalized fluorescein Schiff base FP1 to water samples, sensing membranes and living cells
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 mL stock solution of FP1 was mixed with 10 mL of water samples from tap water (Tianjin, China) and Pan Lake water (Tianjin, China), and different concentrations of Cu2+ solution (Table 1 ). The practical use of FP1 for the detection of Cu2+ in tap water and real lake water samples was evaluated. As shown in Table 1, the percentage recoveries were in the acceptable range from 95% to 103% and the relative standard deviations in percentage (%RSD) values were between 1.7% and 3.3%. This indicates that FP1 effectively sensed Cu2+ in real water samples with excellent accuracy.

FP1 was also successfully applied in sensing membrane. Specifically, the PVDF membrane was flushed evenly with 1 mg mL−1 FP1 solution for 3 min. Then the PVDF membrane was placed on a piece of glass sheet and dried in evacuated container at 25 °C. After drying, the PVDF / FP1 composite film was peeled off the slide to be free-standing. The same procedure was applied to the fabrication of PVDF films as standard. As shown in Fig. 4 , for membrane based on FP1, it was observed that the visible color change and different fluorescence. Cu2+ can cause significant changes in film color and fluorescence, and other equal amounts of ions cannot cause similar changes. This provides methods of onsite rapid qualitative detection of Cu2+.

To demonstrate the applicability of the proposed EDS functionalized fluorescein Schiff base FP1 in living cells, we have employed FP1 for reversible fluorescence sensing of intracellular Cu2+ in cultured VSMCs cells. As is clearly seen from Fig. 5 a–c, loading of 50 μM of FP1 into the VSMCs cell medium results in an intense green fluorescence. This result manifests that the prepared FP1 possesses excellent cell permeability, and is quite suitable for live cell imaging. Interestingly, after incubating the cells with 50 μM Cu2+ for another 30 min, the fluorescence intensity remarkably decreased due to ligand-to-metal charge transfer processes (Fig. 5b-d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of FP1 with previously reported fluorescent sensors for detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The limit of detection, linear dynamic range and application of some fluorescent sensors are compared for detection of Cu2+ in real samples with those obtained in the present study in Table 2 . The limit of detection of the FP1 determining Cu2+ is 0.23 μM, and the detection range is from 0 to 55 μM in this paper. The previously used Cu2+ sensors including rhodamine derivatives [42,43], coumarin derivatives [44], modified carbon dots [45], and quantum dots [46,47] are also listed in Table 2.

In summary, two new fluorescein-based sensors were synthesized successfully via the reaction of Schiff base with fluorescein-monoaldehyde and aniline derivatives bearing EDS or EWS. Due to the electron-donating effects of two methyl groups, FP1 could be easily introduced into the fluorescein and strongly enhanced the fluorescence response to Cu2+ with the lowest detection limit of 0.23 μM in a broader pH working range. 2: 1 FP1-Cu2+ complex was reversible with EDTA. Through the calculation of the frontier orbital, it is proved that when the probe coexists with Cu2+, the complex system is more stable and easier to form. Furthermore, FP1 has been successfully applied to detect Cu2+ in real lake water samples. The sensing membrane and biological samples based on FP1 provided methods of onsite rapid qualitative detection of Cu2+. This study has provided new insight on utilizing the substituents on fluorescein and demonstrated its promising application in environmental monitoring and clinical diagnosis fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409, 51638011 and 51578375), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (18JCYBJC87500, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.111938.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A FRET ratio metric fluorescence sensing system for mercury detection and intracellular colorimetric imaging in live Hela cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Hu, L.L. Hu, M.L. Chen, J.H. Wang, A FRET ratio metric fluorescence sensing system for mercury detection and intracellular colorimetric imaging in live Hela cells, Biosens. Bioelectron. 49 (2013) 499-505.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: NBD-based fluorescent chemo sensor for the selective quantification of copper and sulfide in an aqueous solution and living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Meng, Y. Shi, C. Wang, H. Jia, X. Gao, R. Zhang, Y. Wang, Z. Zhang, NBD-based fluorescent chemo sensor for the selective quantification of copper and sulfide in an aqueous solution and living cells, Org Biomol Chem. 13 (2015) 2918-2926.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Cu-binding site in the cytoplasmic domain of BACE1 identifies a possible link to metal homeostasis and oxidative stress in Alzheimer’s disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Dingwall, A Cu-binding site in the cytoplasmic domain of BACE1 identifies a possible link to metal homeostasis and oxidative stress in Alzheimer's disease, Biochem Soc Trans. 35 (2007) 571-573.
